questions.question: The Opium Wars (1839-42, 1856-60) between China and Western powers opened China to foreign trade. What were the wars fundamentally about?
options: China's refusal to allow Christian missionaries access to Chinese territory
Britain's insistence on continuing to sell opium to China despite the Qing dynasty's ban, combined with China's reluctance to engage in equal trade relationships with Western powers
A territorial dispute over Hong Kong and Macau
Western powers demanding China pay war reparations for attacking foreign merchant ships
questions.answer: Britain's insistence on continuing to sell opium to China despite the Qing dynasty's ban, combined with China's reluctance to engage in equal trade relationships with Western powers
questions.explanation: Britain's East India Company grew opium in India and sold it in China, creating a massive addiction crisis — estimated 10-12 million Chinese addicts by 1839. When China's Commissioner Lin Zexu confiscated and destroyed 1,200 tonnes of opium, Britain went to war. China's military was outclassed by British technology. The Treaties of Nanking (1842) and Tientsin (1858) forced China to open ports, cede Hong Kong, pay reparations, and accept unequal trade terms. China views this as the start of its 'Century of Humiliation' (1839-1949).
questions.memory_hook: Britain went to war to continue selling drugs after China banned them — and won. The Opium Wars opened China to foreign exploitation and began its 'Century of Humiliation.' China's modern foreign policy and Xi Jinping's rhetoric are partly shaped by this memory.

questions.question: The French Revolution produced multiple phases of government. What was the 'Reign of Terror' (1793-1794)?
options: A period of royalist counter-revolution that executed revolutionary leaders
A phase of the Revolution in which the Committee of Public Safety (led by Robespierre) executed approximately 17,000 people and imprisoned 300,000 as suspected enemies of the Revolution
A military campaign that executed captured prisoners of war
The period of Napoleon's military dictatorship following the Revolution
questions.answer: A phase of the Revolution in which the Committee of Public Safety (led by Robespierre) executed approximately 17,000 people and imprisoned 300,000 as suspected enemies of the Revolution
questions.explanation: The Reign of Terror (September 1793-July 1794) saw the guillotine used to execute 'enemies of the Republic' — often on minimal evidence. Maximilien Robespierre's Committee of Public Safety justified the killings as necessary to protect the Revolution. Eventually Robespierre himself was arrested and guillotined on July 28, 1794 (Thermidorian Reaction), ending the Terror. The Revolution devoured its own children — many of its original leaders were executed by their revolutionary successors.
questions.memory_hook: Robespierre said 'terror is the order of the day' — then was guillotined himself. The Revolution's leaders were executed by the next wave of revolutionaries, who were executed by the next wave. The guillotine was democracy's dark mirror: mob justice with no due process.

questions.question: World War II's Pacific Theater began with Japan's attack on Pearl Harbor. Why did Japan attack?
options: Japan mistakenly believed the US was planning to invade Japan in 1942

Japan's imperial expansion into Southeast Asia for oil and resources brought it into conflict with US interests; the US oil embargo (1941) cut off 80% of Japan's oil — forcing Japan to either withdraw from China or go to war
Japan's military leaders calculated they could quickly sue for peace after destroying the US fleet
Germany promised Japan territories in Australia if it neutralized the US Pacific Fleet
questions.answer: Japan's imperial expansion into Southeast Asia for oil and resources brought it into conflict with US interests; the US oil embargo (1941) cut off 80% of Japan's oil — forcing Japan to either withdraw from China or go to war
questions.explanation: Japan invaded China (1937) and Southeast Asia seeking oil, rubber, and rice. The US responded with an oil embargo that cut off 80% of Japan's oil supply. Japan's military calculated it had enough oil reserves for about 2 years — after which it would have to surrender or find oil elsewhere. The attack on Pearl Harbor (December 7, 1941) was intended to neutralize the US Pacific fleet long enough for Japan to seize Dutch East Indies (Indonesia) oil fields. Japan's strategic gamble: America would negotiate rather than fight. They were wrong.
questions.memory_hook: Japan attacked Pearl Harbor because the US oil embargo left them a choice: surrender to American pressure or seize oil fields by force. They chose war, calculating America wouldn't fight. Admiral Yamamoto reportedly said 'I fear we have awakened a sleeping giant.' He had.

questions.question: The 1968 Mexico City Olympics are famous for a political gesture by two American sprinters. What happened?
options: Tommie Smith and John Carlos raised black-gloved fists during the medal ceremony to protest racial inequality in America
Jesse Owens boycotted the opening ceremony to protest segregation in the US South
Muhammad Ali refused to carry the Olympic torch to protest the Vietnam War
The entire US basketball team refused to accept their gold medals to protest racism
questions.answer: Tommie Smith and John Carlos raised black-gloved fists during the medal ceremony to protest racial inequality in America
questions.explanation: Tommie Smith (gold) and John Carlos (bronze) in the 200m stood on the podium with bowed heads and raised black-gloved fists during the US national anthem — a Black Power salute protesting racial injustice. They were immediately suspended by the US Olympic Committee and sent home. Both faced years of threats and career difficulties afterward. The image, photographed by John Dominis, is one of the most iconic protest images of the 20th century. Australian silver medalist Peter Norman wore an Olympic Project for Human Rights badge in solidarity.
questions.memory_hook: Smith and Carlos bowed their heads and raised fists during the national anthem — and were sent home the next day. They faced death threats for decades. Australian silver medalist Norman wore a solidarity badge and was also punished by his country. When Norman died in 2006, Smith and Carlos were pallbearers.

questions.question: Which country has won the most total Olympic gold medals in history (Summer + Winter combined)?
options: China
Soviet Union/Russia combined
United States
Great Britain
questions.explanation: The United States leads all nations in total Olympic gold medals — over 1,000 gold medals in Summer Olympics alone. The Soviet Union was dominant during the Cold War era. In the modern era, the US consistently tops the medal table at Summer Games. China has risen significantly (particularly post-2000), but the US maintains the all-time lead due to its century of Olympic competition. The US also has strong Winter Olympics performance in events like speed skating and skiing.
questions.memory_hook: The US has won more Olympic gold medals than any nation — a product of wealth, population, and a century of systematic sports infrastructure. The Soviet Union was the main rival during the Cold War; China is the current competitive challenger.

questions.question: 'In Cold Blood' (1966) by Truman Capote pioneered the 'true crime' genre. What was it about?
options: The assassination of JFK, reimagined through eyewitness accounts
The 1959 murder of the Clutter family in Holcomb, Kansas by two convicts, and the investigation and execution that followed — reported by Capote as a 'nonfiction novel'
The trial of Al Capone for tax evasion
The unsolved Black Dahlia murder case in Los Angeles
questions.answer: The 1959 murder of the Clutter family in Holcomb, Kansas by two convicts, and the investigation and execution that followed — reported by Capote as a 'nonfiction novel'
questions.explanation: Truman Capote spent 6 years researching and writing In Cold Blood about the murder of Herbert Clutter, his wife, and two children by Perry Smith and Dick Hickock. Capote (and childhood friend Harper Lee) visited Kansas to interview witnesses, investigators, and the killers themselves. Smith and Hickock were executed in 1965. The book pioneered the 'nonfiction novel' — applying novelistic technique (character development, scene-setting, pacing) to true events. Capote claimed the book's success destroyed him — he never finished another major work.
questions.memory_hook: Capote spent 6 years living with this story, interviewing the killers on death row, watching them be executed. Then he claimed the book's success destroyed him — he couldn't write again. In Cold Blood invented modern true crime and cost Capote his creative life.

questions.question: Which playwright wrote 'Death of a Salesman' (1949), often called America's greatest play, and what is its central theme?
options: Eugene O'Neill; the destructiveness of American capitalism on the working class
Arthur Miller; the crushing weight of the American Dream on an ordinary man who cannot achieve it — Willy Loman's delusion vs. reality
Tennessee Williams; the gap between Southern gentility and modern American values
Edward Albee; the illusions couples maintain to preserve their marriages
questions.answer: Arthur Miller; the crushing weight of the American Dream on an ordinary man who cannot achieve it — Willy Loman's delusion vs. reality
questions.explanation: Arthur Miller's Death of a Salesman follows Willy Loman, an aging traveling salesman who cannot accept that he is a failure. The play examines the American Dream's dark side: the promise that success is available to anyone who works hard — and the psychological destruction when that promise proves false. Miller wrote it in a single 24-hour session, almost in its entirety. The play won the Pulitzer Prize and Tony Award. Willy Loman's name is deliberately evocative: 'low man' on the social ladder.
questions.memory_hook: Willy Loman's name means 'low man' — Miller named his protagonist after his theme. The play argues the American Dream destroys people who believe in it most sincerely. Miller wrote it in one 24-hour sitting, saying the whole play was already completely formed in his mind.

questions.question: 'Catch-22' (1961) by Joseph Heller is a satirical novel set during World War II. What is the 'Catch-22' of the title?
options: A military rule that prevents soldiers from being court-martialed for any reason during wartime
The logical paradox: a pilot can be grounded for insanity, but requesting to be grounded proves sanity (since only a sane person would want to avoid danger) — so the request is always denied
A rule requiring soldiers to complete 22 missions before going home, which keeps increasing
A military regulation requiring 22 witnesses to any accusation of misconduct
questions.answer: The logical paradox: a pilot can be grounded for insanity, but requesting to be grounded proves sanity (since only a sane person would want to avoid danger) — so the request is always denied
questions.explanation: Heller's Catch-22 is a satirical WWII novel following bombardier Yossarian who desperately wants to be grounded as insane to avoid dangerous missions. The catch: you can be grounded if you're insane, but asking to be grounded (to avoid death) demonstrates rational self-preservation — therefore sanity — and you must keep flying. The novel's satirical target is bureaucratic logic and the military-institutional absurdity of war. 'Catch-22' entered English as a phrase for any inescapable paradox created by contradictory rules.
questions.memory_hook: Catch-22's paradox: you're crazy if you want to fly dangerous missions, but wanting to stop proves you're sane — so you have to keep flying. Heller invented a phrase that perfectly captures bureaucratic absurdity. Any system where the rules prevent you from escaping the system is a Catch-22.

questions.question: The Mekong River is Southeast Asia's most important river. Through which countries does it flow?
options: India, Bangladesh, Myanmar, Thailand
China, Myanmar, Laos, Thailand, Cambodia, Vietnam
Vietnam, Cambodia, Thailand, Malaysia
China, Mongolia, Russia, Vietnam
questions.answer: China, Myanmar, Laos, Thailand, Cambodia, Vietnam
questions.explanation: The Mekong River rises on the Tibetan Plateau (as the Lancang River in China), flows through Yunnan province, forms the border between Myanmar and Laos, then between Laos and Thailand, passes through Cambodia (where it spreads into the massive Tonle Sap lake system), and enters Vietnam where it forms the Mekong Delta before reaching the South China Sea. The Mekong is critical for 60+ million people's food security (world's largest inland fishery). Chinese dams upstream have significantly reduced downstream flows.
questions.memory_hook: The Mekong flows through 6 countries from Tibet to the South China Sea, sustaining 60+ million people through fisheries. China has built 11 dams on its upper section, reducing water flow downstream — creating food security conflicts between China and Southeast Asian nations.

questions.question: Which African country contains both the Sahara Desert and significant tropical rainforest, making it one of the most geographically diverse nations on the continent?
options: Nigeria
Kenya
Democratic Republic of Congo
Cameroon
questions.explanation: Cameroon is sometimes called 'Africa in miniature' for its extraordinary geographic diversity: Sahel savanna in the north, rainforest in the south and west, Adamawa Highlands in the center, beaches and mangroves on the Atlantic coast, and Mount Cameroon (4,095m, Africa's most active volcano) on the west coast. It borders 6 other countries and has 2 official languages (French and English) — a product of being divided between British and French colonies after WWI.
questions.memory_hook: Cameroon earns 'Africa in miniature' — Sahara fringe, equatorial rainforest, highlands, Atlantic coast, and an active volcano all in one country. Its English/French bilingualism comes from being divided between colonial powers after WWI.

questions.question: What is the ozone layer, where is it, and why does its depletion matter?
options: The ozone layer is at ground level and filters industrial pollution before it reaches humans; depletion increases smog

The ozone layer is in the stratosphere (~15-35 km altitude) and absorbs most of the Sun's ultraviolet B and C radiation, protecting life from DNA damage. Ozone depletion (primarily from CFCs) allowed more UV-B to reach Earth, increasing skin cancer rates
The ozone layer is in the upper thermosphere and has no direct effect on surface life
Ozone depletion is a myth; the hole was created by natural volcanic activity and has no health effects
questions.answer: The ozone layer is in the stratosphere (~15-35 km altitude) and absorbs most of the Sun's ultraviolet B and C radiation, protecting life from DNA damage. Ozone depletion (primarily from CFCs) allowed more UV-B to reach Earth, increasing skin cancer rates
questions.explanation: The stratospheric ozone layer forms when UV radiation splits O₂ molecules; the oxygen atoms recombine into ozone (O₃). Chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs — used in refrigerants and aerosols) catalytically destroy ozone. The Antarctic ozone 'hole' was discovered in 1985. The Montreal Protocol (1987) banned CFCs — one of history's most successful environmental agreements. The ozone layer is slowly recovering and is projected to return to pre-1980 levels by ~2065.
questions.memory_hook: The Montreal Protocol to fix the ozone layer is often cited as the most successful environmental agreement in history — countries actually complied, CFCs were phased out, and the ozone hole is slowly healing. It worked because the substitute chemicals were available and affordable.

questions.question: How does the human eye convert light into the experience of seeing?
options: The eye directly transmits light into the brain, where light itself creates visual experience

Light enters through the cornea and lens (which focus the image), hits the retina (which converts light to electrical signals via rods for low-light and cones for color), and the optic nerve sends these signals to the visual cortex, where the brain constructs the image
The iris directly processes color by filtering specific wavelengths, and the pupil sends electrical signals to the brain
The retina captures a photographic impression that the brain 'reads' like a film negative
questions.answer: Light enters through the cornea and lens (which focus the image), hits the retina (which converts light to electrical signals via rods for low-light and cones for color), and the optic nerve sends these signals to the visual cortex, where the brain constructs the image
questions.explanation: Vision pathway: Light → cornea/lens (focus) → retina (photoreceptors: 120M rods for low-light black/white; 6M cones for color — 3 types: red, green, blue wavelengths) → photopigments (rhodopsin in rods) undergo chemical change → electrical signals via optic nerve → lateral geniculate nucleus → primary visual cortex (V1) → higher visual areas. The brain processes depth, motion, faces, and objects in different specialized regions. Color blindness is usually a cone deficiency. The blind spot is where the optic nerve exits — no photoreceptors.
questions.memory_hook: Your eye doesn't 'take pictures' — it converts light into electricity and sends raw data to the brain, which builds the image. The brain invents what you see, filling in your blind spot and stabilizing the image despite constant eye movement.
